## Summary
Predictive processing (PP) proposes that the brain is fundamentally a prediction machine, constantly generating top-down predictions about sensory input and updating internal models based on bottom-up prediction errors. Conscious perception is the brain's best hypothesis about the causes of its sensory signals, not a passive readout of stimuli.

## Evidence
PP unifies a wide range of phenomena: attention (precision-weighting of prediction errors), perceptual illusions (prior-dominated inference), hallucinations (aberrant prediction error signaling), and active inference (acting to fulfill predictions). Neural evidence includes predictive coding in visual cortex, mismatch negativity in audition, and the hierarchical organization of cortical connectivity (backward connections outnumber forward connections).

## Significance
PP offers a unifying computational framework for perception, action, and cognition. Its philosophical implications are profound: if consciousness is controlled hallucination (Seth, 2021), the boundary between perception and imagination is one of degree, not kind. This connects to phenomenological traditions (Merleau-Ponty's embodied perception) and Buddhist philosophy of mind.
